myths.Agent_1:
Long ago, the sky and the earth were lovers. The Sky, named Aether, was vast and ever-watching. The Earth, named Terra, was vibrant and bound to the ground. They could only touch at the distant horizon, but it was never enough. Aether wished to hold Terra closer, but if he descended, his overwhelming weight would crush her mountains and drown her deep valleys.

Unable to fully embrace his beloved, Aether wept in despair during the quiet night, when the blinding light of the sun did not force him to wear a bright, brave face. His silver tears fell gently upon Terra's cheeks—resting delicately on the green leaves, the blades of grass, and the soft petals of her flowers.

Every morning, Terra awoke to find these glistening droplets scattered across her surface. She knew they were Aether’s tears of longing. To comfort him, she gathered the gentle warmth of the morning sun to lift the droplets, sending them back to his embrace as a soft, invisible mist.

Thus, the morning dew was born. It remains a daily, sparkling reminder of an eternal love, a quiet conversation of longing that forever bridges the heavens and the earth.
myths.Agent_2:
Long before the sky was divided into day and night, the Moon was a perfect, unblemished sphere of polished silver. It hung in the center of the cosmos, serving as a magnificent mirror for the Sun. Every cycle, the Sun would gaze upon its own radiant reflection, ignoring the tiny, flickering Stars that danced in the cosmic dust.

Consumed by envy, the Stars conspired against the Moon. "If the Sun has no mirror," they whispered, "it will have no choice but to look at us."

Gathering fragments of ancient meteors, the Stars hurled a barrage of jagged stones at the silver sphere. They hoped to shatter the Moon entirely. However, the Moon was forged of celestial iron. It did not break, but its pristine surface was deeply bruised and scarred, forever marked by shadowed craters.

When the Sun discovered the battered Moon, it wept tears of golden light. Heartbroken, the Sun refused to share the sky with the jealous Stars ever again. It decreed that it would only shine alone, creating the Day. The Stars were banished to the darkness, forced to keep company only with the scarred Moon, forever reminded of their cruelty during the lonely Night.

Myth: Long ago, the Sun and the Moon shared the twilight sky as eternal lovers. The Sun was a blazing king of gold, and the Moon was a pristine queen of silver. Their combined light cast a perpetual, gentle glow over the young cosmos. But the Stars, cold and ignored in the distant periphery, grew bitter with envy.

Seeking to break this bond, the Stars forged jagged stones from the cosmic void and hurled them at the Moon while the Sun was resting. The Moon’s silver face was deeply bruised and scarred. Ashamed of her new blemishes, she fled into the deep darkness of the void, refusing to let her beloved see her marred reflection.

When the Sun awoke, he found his queen gone. He began a frantic, blazing chase across the heavens, his heat scorching the sky into what we now know as Day. He only catches fleeting glimpses of her pale, scarred face at dawn and dusk. In his grief, the Sun weeps tears of golden fire that fall upon the Earth as the evening mist, a burning reminder of his endless, desperate pursuit to reunite with his lost silver queen.
